findings.summary: logging_utils.py re-exports everything from its split submodules but the split is asymmetric: logging_helpers.py has a sparse stub docstring and an at-function-level import to dodge a circular dependency, while logging_formatters.py and logging_progress.py are full modules — the internal seam is leaky and the circular-import workaround is a convention outlier relative to every other infrastructure submodule
evidence: logging_helpers.py line 7: '# Note: get_logger is imported at function level to avoid circular import' — no other infrastructure module uses this pattern
logging_helpers.py line 43: 'from infrastructure.core.exceptions import TemplateError' inside the function body, unlike all sibling modules which import at top level

logging_utils.py __all__ re-exports EMOJIS, USE_EMOJIS, USE_STRUCTURED_LOGGING, JSONFormatter, TemplateFormatter, Spinner, StreamingProgress — symbols that conceptually belong in their own modules, not the aggregator
findings.suggestion: Move the TemplateError import in format_error_with_suggestions to module level (the circular import it avoids is itself a signal of a dependency direction problem worth resolving) or consolidate logging_helpers into logging_utils if it only exists to hold two helper functions

findings.summary: infrastructure/reporting/__init__.py uses a try/except ImportError block to conditionally define module-level names as None when dashboard_generator is unavailable, which is a unique pattern not used in any sibling __init__.py (validation/__init__.py does straight imports throughout); consumers cannot distinguish 'not installed' from 'explicitly None'
evidence: reporting/__init__.py lines 11-27: generate_all_dashboards, generate_matplotlib_dashboard, generate_plotly_dashboard assigned to None on ImportError
validation/__init__.py: all imports are unconditional, no None-fallback pattern
reporting/__init__.py line 26: 'generate_all_dashboards = None  # type: ignore[assignment]' — mixing callable and None in __all__ is a type-safety outlier
findings.suggestion: Either move optional dashboard symbols out of __all__ and document them as optional, or use importlib.util.find_spec to gate the import and raise a clear ImportError at call time rather than silently setting names to None

findings.summary: PerformanceMonitor.stop() raises BuildError on misuse, but the two context managers that wrap it (performance_context and CodeProfiler.monitor) both use bare 'finally' blocks that call stop() unconditionally — if stop() raises BuildError the original exception is replaced; meanwhile StagePerformanceTracker.end_stage() silently returns {} when not started, choosing a return-empty strategy inconsistent with PerformanceMonitor.stop()'s raise strategy for the same precondition
evidence: performance.py line 103: 'raise BuildError("Monitor not started")' in PerformanceMonitor.stop()
performance.py line 169: 'finally: metrics = monitor.stop()' — exception in stop() during an active exception swaps the active exception
performance.py lines 226-227: 'if self.start_time is None: return {}' in StagePerformanceTracker.end_stage() — same precondition, opposite strategy (silent empty dict vs raise)
findings.suggestion: Align the two tracker classes: either both raise on unstarted state, or both return a sentinel value. In performance_context, guard the stop() call so it does not run when the body raised (use an explicit flag rather than a bare finally).
